title: The Empty Glass
content:
혻혻혻혻There are three lank bards in a borrowed room,
혻혻혻혻Ah! The number is one too few,
혻혻혻혻They have deemed their home and the bars unfit
혻혻혻혻For the thing that they have to do.
혻혻혻혻Three glasses they fill with the Land占퐏 own wine,
혻혻혻혻And the bread of life they pass.
혻혻혻혻Their glasses they take, which they slowly raise,
혻혻혻혻And they drink to an empty glass.

혻혻혻혻(There占퐏 a greater glare in the street to-night,
혻혻혻혻And a louder rush and roar,
혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a mad crowd yelling the winner占퐏 name,
혻혻혻혻And howling the cricket score:
혻혻혻혻Oh! The bright moonlight on the angels white,
혻혻혻혻And the tombs and the monuments grand,
혻혻혻혻And down by the water at Waverley
혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a little lone mound of sand.)

혻혻혻혻Oh, the drinkers would deem them drunk or mad,
혻혻혻혻And the barmaid stare and frown,
혻혻혻혻Each lays a hand on the empty glass
혻혻혻혻Ere they turn it upside down.
혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a name they know, in a hand they know,
혻혻혻혻Was scratched with a diamond there,
혻혻혻혻And they place it in sight, turn on more light,
혻혻혻혻And they fill their glasses fair.

혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a widow that weeps by the Hornsby line,
혻혻혻혻And she stood by him long and true,
혻혻혻혻But the widow should think by the Hornsby line
혻혻혻혻That others have loved him too,
혻혻혻혻占폯was a peaceful end, and his work was done,
혻혻혻혻When called with the year away;
혻혻혻혻And the greatest lady in all the land
혻혻혻혻Is working for her to-day.

혻혻혻혻If the widow should fear for her children占퐏 fate,
혻혻혻혻Or brood on a future lot,
혻혻혻혻In a frivolous land with her widowed state
혻혻혻혻In a short twelve months forgot.
혻혻혻혻She can lay her down for a peaceful rest
혻혻혻혻And forget her grief in sleep,
혻혻혻혻For his brothers have taken an oath to-night,
혻혻혻혻An oath that their hearts can keep.

혻혻혻혻They have taken an oath to his memory,
혻혻혻혻A pledge they cannot recall,
혻혻혻혻To stand by the woman that stood by him,
혻혻혻혻Through poverty, illness and all.
혻혻혻혻They are young men yet, or the prime of life,
혻혻혻혻And as each lays down his trust,
혻혻혻혻May the world be kind to the left behind,
혻혻혻혻And their native land be just.

혻혻혻혻(Silence of death in town to-night,
혻혻혻혻And the streets seem strangely clear,
혻혻혻혻Have the pitiful slaves of the gambling curse
혻혻혻혻Fled home for a strange new fear?
혻혻혻혻Oh, the soft moonlight on the angels white,
혻혻혻혻Where the beautiful marbles stand,
혻혻혻혻And down by the rollers at Waverley
혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a mound of the golden sand.)
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
